title: Domain Policies and Rules Knowledge Check

questions.question: Which rules can be set within the Policy Administrator user interface?
options: Access control, ad hoc access, and agreements
Access control, indexing, and notification
Access control, security labels, and agreements
Ad hoc access, indexing, and notification
questions.rationale: The Policy Administrator manages access control, indexing, and notification rules. Ad hoc access is set through the Edit Access Control dialog on individual objects. Agreements and security labels are configured separately.

questions.question: What are the elements used to define an access control rule?
options: Domain, object version, participants, and permissions
Domain, object type, lifecycle state, participants, and permissions
Object type, participants, permissions, and agreement
Domain, object type, security label, and permissions
questions.rationale: Five components define a domain access control rule: Domain (where), Object type (what), Lifecycle state (when), Participants (who), and Permissions (how). Object version and security labels are not components of domain rules.

questions.question: If a participant is GRANTED a permission by one ACL but DENIED the same permission by another ACL, what is the result?
options: The permission is granted because grants take precedence
The permission is NOT granted
The system uses the most recent rule
The participant must request access through an agreement
questions.rationale: When a participant is both granted and denied the same permission by different ACLs, the permission is NOT granted. The deny takes precedence over the grant in Windchill's ACL resolution principles.

questions.question: Which access control approach is considered the most secure?
options: Deny-based: grant broad access, then deny specific groups
Inverse deny: use 'all except participant' rules
Grant-only: remove broad grants, explicitly grant each group
User-specific: create individual rules for each user
questions.rationale: The grant-only approach is most secure because access is forbidden by default. Each grant is explicit, there are fewer rules, and the approach simplifies reading and maintaining access control. User-specific rules are too granular for practical administration.

questions.question: What happens when a product's Private Access is set to Yes?
options: All team members lose access to the product
The product's domain moves under the Private domain, bypassing PDM rules
Security labels are automatically applied to all objects
The product is deleted from the organization
questions.rationale: Setting Private Access to Yes moves the product's Default domain to be a child of the Private domain instead of the PDM domain. This bypasses any global rules in the PDM domain, hiding released objects from non-team members.
